name: Fearow
region: Kanto, Johto, Sinnoh, Unova, Kalos, Alola, and long-route aerial corridors
route: Kanto Routes 9, 16, 17, 18, 23 and Sevii Island paths; Johto Routes 9, 10, 16, 17, 18, 22, 42, 47, and 48; Sinnoh Routes 225, 226, 227, and Stark Mountain; Unova Route 15 records; Kalos Victory Road records; Alola Route 10, Route 17, Mount Hokulani, and Poni Plains
habitat: Open grassland, canyon roads, riverbanks, coastlines, mountain slopes, cliff thermals, long route corridors, farms, rocky islands, and high perches overlooking prey movement
classification: Beak Pokemon / Final Stage Endurance Raptor
behaviorTemperament: Aggressive, watchful, and stamina-driven. Fearow patrol broad ranges, dive from height, snatch food, and retreat skyward when threatened. They are less noisy than Spearow but far more dangerous on contact.
diet: Fish, insects, rodents, small birds, eggs, grubs, field prey, carrion, stolen food, and smaller Pokemon taken from grass, soil, or shallow water
activityPattern: Diurnal with dawn patrols and long midday soaring. Coastal and mountain individuals may ride thermals for hours while scanning for prey.
bestTimeToFind: Morning or late afternoon above long roads, cliffs, route signs, coastal shallows, river bends, and fields where small prey flushes into open air
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Large silhouette is visible, but distance and altitude make age, intent, and target hard to judge until it dives.
conservationStatus: Locally protected as an aerial predator, but controlled near airports, livestock, poultry yards, and heavily traveled route markets
populationStatus: Stable in open country and route corridors. Less common than Spearow due to larger territory needs and competition with Pidgeot and other raptors.
description: A Normal and Flying-type beak Pokemon with broad wings, strong stamina, and a long piercing beak used to strike prey from air, soil, and water.
lore: Old route maps in Kanto marked certain cliffs not with warning signs for falling rock, but with painted beaks. Those were Fearow perches. Caravans learned not to carry uncovered food through Route 17 and Route 18 when high shadows circled overhead. Unlike Spearow, Fearow does not need a flock to make travelers retreat. It rises, watches, chooses a target, and drops. Johto records add another detail: Fearow remember profitable paths. A bird that steals fish from a bridge today may return tomorrow at the same hour. In Alola, fish Pokemon and coastline traffic made Fearow a problem around open markets and shore paths. Ancient murals resembling Fearow suggest humans have watched this body plan for a very long time. In real-world field terms, Fearow is not just a big Spearow. It is the line's answer to distance, altitude, and hunger. If Spearow is the alarm, Fearow is the spear falling from it.
survivalUse: Can reveal prey movement, thermal columns, water edges, and unsafe open corridors. Following Fearow from a distance may lead to fish, carrion, or river shallows, but food theft risk is high.
handlingWarning: Do not handle without raptor equipment. Keep food covered, protect eyes and face, and never stand under a low circling Fearow with exposed small animals or shiny objects.
encounterAdvice: Move under cover, secure food, and avoid running in open ground. If it dives, crouch beside a tree, vehicle, rock, or pack frame rather than waving arms in open air.
firstAid: For beak punctures, control bleeding, irrigate thoroughly, dress the wound, and seek urgent care for face, eye, neck, tendon, or deep muscle injuries. Treat falls caused by panic as secondary trauma.
companionSuitability: Poor for casual companionship; suitable only for expert aerial handlers, conservation teams, or licensed long-range field scouts
fieldBehaviors: Soars for long periods without landing
Plucks prey from soil or shallow water
Uses a long beak for precise strikes
fieldNote: Fearow that circle high and wide are usually searching. Fearow that fly low, silent, and straight have already chosen a route or target.
originStory: Fearow evolves from Spearow when short-winged territorial aggression becomes too costly for a growing bird. Evolution at Level 20 expands the wing area, lengthens the neck, sharpens the beak into a deep probing tool, and shifts the animal from grass defense to aerial patrol. The line likely thrived along old travel routes where insects, rodents, fish, and human food were concentrated. Cliff paths, canyon roads, river crossings, and islands all rewarded birds that could fly longer than rivals and strike from above before prey reached cover.

evolutionLine.name: Spearow
evolutionLine.note: Base stage tiny bird Pokemon. Evolves when wing endurance, beak strength, and territorial drive become strong enough for long patrols.

evolutionLine.name: Fearow
evolutionLine.note: Adult beak Pokemon. Built for distance flight, high dives, riverbank hunting, and aggressive aerial territory defense.

mainLine.name: Spearow
mainLine.note: Small territorial grassland bird. Loud alarm calls, rapid wingbeats, and reckless pecking protect its range.

mainLine.name: Fearow
mainLine.note: Long-beaked endurance raptor. Patrols roads, cliffs, fields, and coastlines while hunting insects, fish, and small prey.

domesticationStatus: Mostly wild; licensed use in aerial scouting and pest control is possible but demanding
prey: Fish, rodents, insects, grubs, small birds, Rattata, nestlings, eggs, carrion, and exposed traveler food
temperamentTriggers: Small animals exposed in open routes
territoryRange: Usually 5 to 20 square miles depending on cliffs, food, and wind. Coastal or mountain birds may patrol longer corridors following thermals.
lifeCycle: Spearow evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20. Adults claim high perches, broad hunting lanes, and nesting ledges, then patrol by sight over fields, rivers, roads, and shorelines.
containmentRequirements: Large licensed aviary or mews, high perches, reinforced flight space, safe weathering area, raptor-grade gloves, controlled feeding stations, injury monitoring, and no public access to feeding zones
ecologicalRole: Controls rodents, insects, fish overpopulation in shallow areas, and carrion waste. Also pressures smaller bird Pokemon and shapes open-route animal movement.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Low to moderate. Main concerns are feather parasites, beak injuries, contaminated prey, and puncture wound infection after attacks.
reproductionNesting: Nests on cliffs, tall trees, route bluffs, canyon ledges, and high human structures. Adults defend airspace with dives and may abandon nests if repeated human disturbance continues.
predators: Pidgeot, large electric types, stronger aerial predators, humans, rock-type ambush predators near cliffs, and rival Fearow during territorial conflict
emergencyResponse: For repeated dive attacks, move under cover, cover the head, secure food, and leave the territory. Do not fire projectiles upward unless legally authorized and necessary for immediate defense.
legalStatus: Often protected as a native aerial predator, but controlled permits may apply around airports, food markets, poultry yards, and high-traffic route corridors.
stressSicknessSigns: Loss of balance on high perch
lifespan: Wild: 6 to 12 years. Managed care: 18 to 25 years with expert raptor housing and flight conditioning.
socialStructure: Mostly pairs or solitary hunters outside breeding season. Juveniles may linger near Spearow flocks, but adults defend separate hunting lanes.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Main hazards are dive force, beak puncture, talon grip, and food snatching.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Dive hazard can begin from hundreds of feet above, with contact risk at the final 3 to 10 ft.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast dive and pursuit flight, roughly 45 to 70 mph in field conditions
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Excellent in open sky, weaker in dense brush or tight indoor spaces
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Ground jump is limited, but takeoff is powerful from perches and ledges
fieldCapabilities.balance: Strong perch grip and long tail control during dives and high wind

trainingRisk: Severe puncture wounds, face strikes, food aggression, escape, wing injuries, stress from cramped housing, and handler overconfidence
physiology: Fearow has a long neck, broad endurance wings, strong flight muscles, keen eyes, and a long beak shaped for probing soil or water and piercing prey. Its stamina allows all-day flight, making simple distance an unreliable safety measure.

keyFacts.wingspan: Approx. 5 to 7 ft in field specimens
keyFacts.lifestyle: Diurnal aerial predator and route patrol bird
keyFacts.diet: Carnivorous-leaning omnivore with fish and small prey focus
keyFacts.habitat: Cliffs, open routes, rivers, coastlines, farms, canyons, and mountain roads
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 6 to 12 years. Managed: 18 to 25 years.
keyFacts.groupSize: Usually solitary adult or mated pair

domesticationDifficulty: Very high. Requires expert raptor handling, large flight space, strict feeding control, and public-safety planning.
physicalCharacteristics: Large brown bird Pokemon with broad wings, cream flight-feather tips, shaggy neck base, long pink spear-like beak, red head crest, narrow eyes, strong legs, and long tail feathers.
dietRestrictions: Avoid spoiled fish, lead-contaminated prey, high-salt human food, excessive fatty scraps, cramped feeding cages, and sudden diet changes that reduce flight condition.

attacks.name: Drill Run
attacks.fieldUse: Diving beak-and-body attack used to strike grounded targets or dig prey from soft soil.

attacks.name: Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Precise beak strike for insects, fish, or warning jabs.

attacks.name: Pluck
attacks.fieldUse: Snatch attack used on exposed food, berries, and small prey.

attacks.name: Fury Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Repeated stabbing strikes at close range.

attacks.name: Aerial Ace
attacks.fieldUse: Clean aerial cut from a sudden dive or side pass.

attacks.name: Wing Attack
attacks.fieldUse: Forceful wing blow during low passes.

attacks.name: Drill Peck
attacks.fieldUse: Signature deep piercing attack from the long beak.

attacks.name: Roost
attacks.fieldUse: Perch recovery behavior after long patrols or wing strain.

Kanto: Sevii Island areas including Canyon Entrance, Cape Brink, Kindle Road, Mt. Ember, Ruin Valley, Sevault Canyon, Treasure Beach, and Water Path
Open roads and cliff-adjacent route corridors
Johto: High route edges and open grassland hunting lanes
Alola: Routes 10 and 17, Mount Hokulani, and Poni Plains

officialEvolutionSummary.spearowToFearow: Spearow evolves into Fearow starting at Level 20.
officialEvolutionSummary.finalStage: Fearow is the final stage of the Spearow line.
officialEvolutionSummary.specialForm: No standard Mega Evolution or regional form is currently part of the main line.

sourceUseNote: Core typing, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, game location anchors, and move names are based on official-style Pokedex and game database records. Lifespan, ecology, first aid, containment, and real-world field behavior are interpretive survival-guide material for this passion project.
